disclaimer.legalText: This is a self-guided educational audio walk for independent visitors. It is not an official guided tour, not a licensed tourist guide service, and it is not affiliated with the Hellenic Ministry of Culture, the City of Athens, or any official archaeological authority.
disclaimer.safetyText: Visitors are responsible for their own safety. Check weather conditions, wear suitable footwear, carry water, avoid extreme heat, and follow all local rules and signs.

hero.title: PNYX
hero.subtitle: The Hidden Site of Athenian Democracy
hero.support: Just 20 minutes from the Acropolis, discover the overlooked hill where Athenians gathered to speak, debate and decide.

free.title: Free discovery
free.body: Introduction + Why Almost Everyone Misses the Pnyx

about.heading: About the Pnyx
about.body: The Pnyx is the hill in central Athens where the citizens' assembly gathered for over 200 years. While millions visit the Acropolis each year, the Pnyx — a short walk away — remains largely unknown. This walk tells the story of the place where democracy was not just invented, but practised.

aboutTeam.heading: Who made this?
aboutTeam.body: PNYX Athens is a personal project by two friends — no company, no tour agency. We built it because we believe this hill deserves to be more than a footnote.

signup.heading: The extended version coming soon
signup.subhead: Get notified when the full 45-minute walk launches.

about.title: Who's Behind PNYX Athens
about.photoAlt: The two creators of PNYX Athens
about.intro: We're two friends who share a love of history and a slight obsession with places everyone walks past. When we first climbed the Pnyx, we couldn't believe how empty it was — the birthplace of democratic practice, a short walk from the Acropolis, and almost no one there. So we decided to build the guide we wished we'd had.

premium.title: Go deeper on the Pnyx

howItWorks.title: How it works
howItWorks.intro: PNYX Athens is a self-guided audio experience. No app, no booking, no group — just you, your phone and the hill where democracy was practised.

step1.title: Listen to the introduction
step1.body: Start anywhere — at your hotel, in a café or on the way. The introduction and the first chapter are completely free.

step2.title: Walk to the Pnyx
step2.body: It's about a 20-minute walk from the Acropolis. Use the built-in directions or Google Maps to find the way.

step3.title: Experience chapter 1 on site
step3.body: Stand where the Athenian assembly met and listen to the story of why almost everyone misses this place.

step4.title: Go deeper if you want more
step4.body: Unlock the remaining chapters and bonus stories with a one-time payment — or by supporting the project with a donation.

story.title: Our story
story.p1: We're two friends who share a love of history and a slight obsession with places everyone walks past. When we first climbed the Pnyx, we couldn't believe how empty it was — the birthplace of democratic practice, a short walk from the Acropolis, and almost no one there.
story.p2: So we decided to build the guide we wished we'd had: an audio experience that lets you stand on the speakers' rock and understand what actually happened here.
story.p3: PNYX Athens is independent — no company, no tour agency. If it gives you even one moment where the ancient assembly feels real, it has done its job.

contact.title: Contact
contact.body: Questions, feedback, ideas or corrections — we'd genuinely love to hear from you. We read every message.

gallery.title: Photos of the Pnyx 🏛
gallery.attributionNote: Photos are linked from external sources and are not hosted by this app.

stop1.title: Why almost everyone misses the Pnyx
stop1.description: Most visitors come to Athens for the Acropolis. But a short walk away stands a quieter hill where one of the most important political experiments in human history took shape. We start here — at the edge of the site — to understand why it has been forgotten, and why that matters.

stop2.title: Where the Athenian Assembly met
stop2.description: This was the meeting place of the ekklesia, the citizens' assembly of ancient Athens. Here, thousands of citizens gathered to debate, vote and decide on matters of war, law and public life. The bema — the speakers' rock — still stands here. Stand on the same ground they stood on.

stop3.title: Who was allowed to speak — and who was excluded
stop3.description: Athenian democracy was revolutionary, but it was not equal by modern standards. Women, enslaved people and foreigners were excluded from political participation. Understanding who was excluded tells us as much about democracy as understanding who participated.

stop4.title: What democracy meant then — and what it means now
stop4.description: The Pnyx reminds us that democracy was not born as a finished system. It was debated, limited, expanded and challenged — just as democracy still is today. As you leave this hill, consider: what would it mean to take that seriously now?

finish.heading: Walk complete.
finish.subhead: You've stood where Athenian citizens debated war, law, and the future of their city. Not many tourists make it here.
finish.feedbackSubhead: Your feedback shapes the next version.
finish.signupSubhead: A 45-minute walk with deeper historical context. Be first to know.

sections.heading: 1. Who we are
paragraphs: PNYX Athens is an independent educational project. We are not affiliated with any official tourism body, municipality, or cultural institution.

sections.heading: 2. What data we collect
paragraphs: Email address: If you choose to sign up for updates, we collect your email address and a record of your consent.
Feedback: If you submit feedback, we collect your star rating, optional written comments, and your answer to the "would you pay" question.

Analytics events: We collect anonymous usage events (e.g. page views, audio plays) to understand how visitors use the walk. We do not use third-party advertising trackers. No personally identifiable information is linked to analytics events.

sections.heading: 3. How we use your data
paragraphs: We use your email address only to send you updates about this walk when you have explicitly consented. We use feedback and analytics to improve the experience. We do not sell, share, or rent your data to third parties.

sections.heading: 4. Data processor
paragraphs: Your data is stored in Supabase, a cloud database service. Supabase acts as a data processor on our behalf. Data is stored in the EU region.

sections.heading: 5. Your rights (GDPR)
paragraphs: Under GDPR, you have the right to access, correct, or delete your personal data at any time. To exercise these rights, contact us. You may unsubscribe from emails at any time by clicking the unsubscribe link in any email we send.

sections.heading: 6. Retention
paragraphs: We retain email signup data for as long as you remain subscribed. Feedback and analytics data is retained indefinitely for product improvement purposes and contains no personally identifiable information.

sections.heading: 1. Nature of this service
paragraphs: PNYX Athens is a self-guided educational audio walk for independent visitors. It is not an official guided tour, not a licensed tourist guide service, and it is not affiliated with the Hellenic Ministry of Culture, the City of Athens, or any official archaeological authority.

sections.heading: 2. Visitor responsibility
paragraphs: By using this walk, you confirm that you are visiting the Pnyx independently and at your own risk. You are responsible for your own safety, including checking weather conditions, wearing appropriate footwear, carrying water, and following all signs and regulations at the site.

sections.heading: 3. Accuracy of content
paragraphs: The historical information in this walk is provided for educational purposes. While we aim for accuracy, we make no warranty as to the completeness or correctness of the content. For authoritative information, consult academic sources or the official Hellenic Ministry of Culture.

sections.heading: 4. Limitation of liability
paragraphs: PNYX Athens accepts no liability for any injury, loss, or damage that occurs during your visit to the Pnyx or as a result of following this walk. Use this service at your own discretion.

sections.heading: 5. Intellectual property
paragraphs: The content of this walk, including text and audio, is the property of PNYX Athens. You may not reproduce or distribute it without permission. Historical facts in the public domain are not claimed as proprietary.

sections.heading: 6. Changes to terms
paragraphs: We may update these terms from time to time. Continued use of the service after changes constitutes acceptance of the revised terms.

sectionsBeforeTable.heading: What are cookies?
paragraphs: Cookies are small text files stored on your device by your browser. This website uses a minimal number of cookies necessary for the service to function.

sectionsAfterTable.heading: What we do NOT use
paragraphs: We do not use advertising cookies, social media tracking cookies, or any third-party analytics cookies. The only cookies set by this site are those strictly necessary for the admin authentication system to function.

sectionsAfterTable.heading: Visitors (non-admin)
paragraphs: If you are using this app as a visitor (not logging into the admin area), no cookies are set on your device. Analytics events are collected as anonymous server-side records with no cookie tracking.

sectionsAfterTable.heading: Managing cookies
paragraphs: You can manage or delete cookies through your browser settings. Deleting the authentication cookie will log you out of the admin area. It has no effect on the visitor experience.
